caveats: a: Occupation mix is judgment-adjusted from a 4-digit NAICS aggregate (541200) that includes CPA/tax-prep/bookkeeping establishments, not measured directly for 541214; BLS OEWS does not publish occupational detail at the 541214 6-digit level.

a: The BLS occupational-decline figure for Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks is a net employment projection (net of business growth, consolidation, and automation combined), not a pure task-exposure measure, so it overstates AI-specific exposure to an unknown degree.

a: This proxy uses a coarser NAICS level than the QCEW-sourced, 541214-specific wage data underlying the dataset-injected labor-share input, so the two are not drawn from perfectly matched populations even though both nominally describe this industry.

rho: No direct survey of AI-adoption timelines among independent payroll bureaus was found; this is bounded judgment built from adjacent evidence (vendor roadmaps, liability structure), not a measured implementation rate.

rho: Faster large-vendor AI shipping could pull small-bureau adoption forward, or could instead accelerate disintermediation of bureaus entirely (captured in `o`) rather than being 'implemented' by the bureau itself; that risk is carried in `o`, not double-counted here.

e: No public data source enumerates what share of 541214 LMM-band firms are franchise-affiliated, reseller/agent-only, or otherwise structurally impaired for a clean control transfer; this is judgment.

e: Franchise agreements are not necessarily disqualifying under the lens (a franchise territory can still undergo a genuine control transfer via resale to a new franchisee), but they add transaction friction and consent risk not present in fully independent bureaus.
s5: Owner-age evidence is drawn from CPA/accounting firms, not payroll bureaus specifically -- a population proxy, not a direct measurement.

s5: Deal-flow evidence is drawn substantially from one acquirer (Vensure/PrismHR) whose targets blend PEO/ASO and payroll-processing business models; the pure-541214 slice of that activity is not separately reported.

s5: The Rosenberg survey figures are from a 2018 publication; more recent AICPA retirement-wave commentary suggests the skew has not reversed, but current-year percentages are not independently confirmed here.

q: No bureau-level renewal-price panel was found; the qualitative claim of price/margin compression is well supported, but the specific retained-share number is bounded judgment, not a measured pass-through rate.

q: PEPM pricing ranges cited are current retail figures for the small-business market broadly, not specifically for LMM-band, more-complex clients, who likely see less commoditized pricing and possibly higher retention.

d5: Market-size trackers used here are not consistently scoped (some blend PEO, HCM-software, and payroll-processing revenue); the 541214-specific portion of demand is not separately reported by any source found.
d5: This is a nominal-dollar-market proxy for a real-quantity construct; the price/volume decomposition is judgment, not measured.
o: No direct measurement of the self-serve/embedded share of total industry volume was found; both bounding forces are well documented qualitatively but the net split is judgment.

o: Legal liability remaining with the employer is a reason clients might value an accountable operator's expertise, but it is not proof they will keep paying for one -- some clients may accept the residual liability themselves in exchange for lower self-serve pricing, which would pull this below the base case.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring subscription-like billing to external small- and mid-size business clients, almost always per-employee-per-month (PEPM, typically a small monthly base fee plus roughly $5-17 per employee per month) or per-payroll-run fees tied to headcount and complexity (multi-state withholding, garnishments, benefits deductions). Billing is fixed/volume-linked rather than cost-plus or hourly, with contract renewal (commonly annual) as the main repricing event.
lens.excluded_activities: Offices of Certified Public Accountants and other accounting/tax-prep/bookkeeping establishments for which payroll is incidental to a broader accounting practice (541211/541213/541219, which BLS/Census group with 541214 under NAICS 541200 but which are excluded from this lens); Professional Employer Organizations and other co-employment/employee-leasing arrangements (NAICS 561330) even though economically adjacent and sometimes bundled with payroll bureaus commercially; in-house corporate payroll departments; and pure payroll software publishers with no service-bureau client relationship (NAICS 511210-type activity).
lens.included_activities: Establishments primarily engaged in collecting client hours-worked, pay-rate, and deduction data and using it to generate paychecks, payroll registers/reports, and payroll tax filings/deposits for external client businesses on a recurring, repeat basis, per the NAICS 541214 definition. Includes independently owned and franchise-model boutique payroll bureaus (e.g., locally operated territories of national payroll-franchise concepts) operating in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.

a.bridge: Source population is BLS OEWS national occupation employment/wage estimates for NAICS 541200 (Accounting, Tax Preparation, Bookkeeping, and Payroll Services) -- confirmed via the BLS OEWS national industry list to be the finest NAICS level BLS publishes occupational detail for; no separate 541214 breakout exists. That 4-digit aggregate is dominated by Accountants and Auditors (31.7% of the group's 1.1M employment, $93k mean wage) -- a higher-skill, more judgment-intensive occupation than the ones that actually staff a stand-alone payroll-processing bureau. Transformation: reweight toward the occupation mix judged representative of a payroll-service establishment specifically (payroll/timekeeping clerks, bookkeeping/accounting clerks, customer service reps, data entry, with smaller software-support and sales/management shares), informed by O*NET task content for Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks (43-3051.00) and by BLS Employment Projections showing that occupation shrinking 16.7% (161,100 to 134,200) between 2024 and 2034 -- one of the steepest declines of any occupation BLS tracks, versus +3.1% for all occupations. Direction of error: using the unadjusted 541200 mix would understate `a` for actual payroll-services establishments, since it dilutes the payroll-clerical core with less-automatable CPA/audit work.
caveats: Occupation mix is judgment-adjusted from a 4-digit NAICS aggregate (541200) that includes CPA/tax-prep/bookkeeping establishments, not measured directly for 541214; BLS OEWS does not publish occupational detail at the 541214 6-digit level.

The BLS occupational-decline figure for Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks is a net employment projection (net of business growth, consolidation, and automation combined), not a pure task-exposure measure, so it overstates AI-specific exposure to an unknown degree.

This proxy uses a coarser NAICS level than the QCEW-sourced, 541214-specific wage data underlying the dataset-injected labor-share input, so the two are not drawn from perfectly matched populations even though both nominally describe this industry.
a.change_evidence: A BLS OEWS or Employment Projections special tabulation breaking NAICS 5412 down to the 541214 6-digit level (or a commercial staffing-pattern extract, e.g. Lightcast, at that granularity) would move this from PROXY to OBSERVED. A time-and-motion or task-time-allocation study inside actual mid-size payroll bureaus (vendor-supplied or via a trade body such as the National Payroll Reporting Consortium / PayrollOrg) would sharpen the wage-weighting materially.
a.rationale: Core production work in a payroll bureau -- timecard/hours intake, wage and deduction calculation, paycheck/report generation, standard tax-filing preparation, first-line client support -- is structured, rules-based, and already partially automated by conventional (pre-AI) payroll software; AI/LLM tooling extends that into exception handling, anomaly detection, natural-language client Q&A, and reconciliation write-ups. BLS's own long-range occupational projections already price in a large piece of this: Payroll and Timekeeping Clerks is one of very few occupations nationally projected to shrink by double digits over the next decade, which BLS commentary and industry sources attribute chiefly to software/automation displacement. Judgment-heavy, liability-bearing, or relationship-dependent tasks (new-client onboarding advice, non-standard payroll situations, tax-notice defense, sales) are judged materially less exposed, which keeps the base below the occupation-level decline rate.

d5.bridge: Source population is total US payroll-services/payroll-outsourcing market-size and growth research (Mordor Intelligence: USD 8.44B in 2025 to USD 11.61B by 2031, 5.47% CAGR; separately, IBISWorld and other trackers cite figures ranging from roughly USD 15.8B to USD 80.9B for overlapping but inconsistently scoped definitions of 'payroll services,' reflecting differing inclusion of adjacent HCM-software and PEO revenue). Transformation: these are nominal-dollar market-size CAGRs, which blend real volume growth, price change, and scope drift across trackers, while d5 requires constant-price, constant-quality quantity growth for the current service basket specifically. Direction of error: because per-employee pricing is under visible downward competitive pressure (aggressive entrant pricing from embedded-payroll infrastructure and self-serve SaaS), real quantity growth is plausibly at or above the nominal CAGR -- these nominal figures likely understate real volume growth of the underlying basket -- partially offset by the fact that some of the 'growth' in these market-size figures reflects platforms/features outside the strict 541214 service basket (HCM add-ons, benefits admin, earned-wage access) rather than the core payroll-processing basket itself.
caveats: Market-size trackers used here are not consistently scoped (some blend PEO, HCM-software, and payroll-processing revenue); the 541214-specific portion of demand is not separately reported by any source found.
This is a nominal-dollar-market proxy for a real-quantity construct; the price/volume decomposition is judgment, not measured.
d5.change_evidence: A volume-based industry measure (e.g., number of payrolls processed, or number of W-2/1099 filings processed by third-party reporting agents, from IRS/SSA administrative data) would give a real quantity series independent of price; a single, consistently-scoped market-size tracker followed over multiple years (rather than a cross-vendor comparison) would remove the scope-drift noise seen here.
d5.rationale: Underlying secular demand drivers are durable and mostly regulatory: expanding multi-state and remote-work tax/withholding complexity, new state paid-leave and pay-transparency mandates, and continued small-business formation all push more employers toward needing payroll processed correctly across more jurisdictions, not fewer. These are structural rather than cyclical. Against that, nominal market-growth estimates across trackers cluster in the mid-single-digit percent per year range, which nets to meaningful but not explosive five-year compounding once price effects are stripped out.

caveats: No public data source enumerates what share of 541214 LMM-band firms are franchise-affiliated, reseller/agent-only, or otherwise structurally impaired for a clean control transfer; this is judgment.

Franchise agreements are not necessarily disqualifying under the lens (a franchise territory can still undergo a genuine control transfer via resale to a new franchisee), but they add transaction friction and consent risk not present in fully independent bureaus.
e.change_evidence: A count of franchise-affiliated payroll bureaus (Payroll Vault and comparable concepts) versus fully independent bureaus, and typical franchise-transfer terms (consent rights, right of first refusal, non-competes), would sharpen the low end of this range; a SUSB/Census employer-vs-nonemployer split within 541214 would clarify how many of the smallest units are single-person referral shops rather than operating bureaus.
e.rationale: NAICS 541214 is already defined narrowly around establishments that provide payroll processing to external clients and explicitly excludes CPA offices, so the definitional risk of counting captive/internal payroll departments is low relative to broader NAICS codes. The main leakage is (1) franchise-model bureaus (e.g., Payroll Vault, a nationally franchised, low-fixed-cost, recurring-PEPM-revenue local-territory concept) where the operating business is a real, sellable client relationship but transfer may require franchisor consent or be structured as a territory resale rather than a clean asset/stock sale, and (2) referral/reseller arrangements (agents who sell but do not operate an ADP/Paychex-branded service) that may not constitute a standalone operating business with independent pricing power. Absent a franchise-penetration estimate for this segment, most of the national firm population is judged to clear the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541214 ($22,102,632,622) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541214 ($27,322,843,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $98,054 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541214); class <5: 2,110 firms, avg revenue/firm $581,493 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $91,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 662 firms, avg revenue/firm $919,816 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $143,951 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 480 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,588,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $248,602 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 741 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,599,022 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $719,747 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 297 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,560,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,122,240 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 194 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,119,103 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,947,140 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 297

caveats: No direct measurement of the self-serve/embedded share of total industry volume was found; both bounding forces are well documented qualitatively but the net split is judgment.

Legal liability remaining with the employer is a reason clients might value an accountable operator's expertise, but it is not proof they will keep paying for one -- some clients may accept the residual liability themselves in exchange for lower self-serve pricing, which would pull this below the base case.
o.change_evidence: Vendor-disclosed mix-shift data (share of new payroll relationships initiated as embedded/self-serve versus full-service-with-a-human-bureau) tracked over several years; a segmentation study of which client types (by headcount, state count, industry) churn to self-serve versus remain with a bureau.
o.rationale: Two offsetting forces bound this. Pulling down: self-serve SaaS (Gusto alone reports 400,000+ direct small-business customers) and embedded-payroll infrastructure (Check, Gusto Embedded, Zeal, Salsa, ADP Embedded) increasingly let a business, or the vertical software it already uses, run payroll directly with no ongoing human-staffed bureau relationship at all -- a real disintermediation channel concentrated at the simplest, single-state, low-headcount end of the market. Holding it up: regardless of which vendor or software a client uses, the employer (and, per the IRS Trust Fund Recovery Penalty, potentially its owners, officers, and even bookkeepers/controllers personally) remains legally liable for payroll-tax deposit accuracy and timing -- outsourcing does not transfer that liability -- which sustains willingness to pay for an accountable third party's expertise and risk-absorption, especially for multi-state, benefits-heavy, or otherwise complex clients not well served by pure self-serve tools. Net, a majority but a declining majority of year-5 quantity is judged to still require an accountable operator of the lens's kind.

caveats: No bureau-level renewal-price panel was found; the qualitative claim of price/margin compression is well supported, but the specific retained-share number is bounded judgment, not a measured pass-through rate.

PEPM pricing ranges cited are current retail figures for the small-business market broadly, not specifically for LMM-band, more-complex clients, who likely see less commoditized pricing and possibly higher retention.
q.change_evidence: Bureau-level renewal-price and gross-margin trend data (e.g., from a business broker's completed-deal file, or a trade-association benchmarking survey) showing realized price/margin change after a bureau adopts a specific automation feature; embedded-payroll provider disclosures on PEPM pricing trend versus prior years.
q.rationale: Billing in this industry is overwhelmingly a recurring per-employee-per-month (PEPM, roughly $5-17/employee plus a small monthly base) or per-payroll-run fee -- a fixed/volume-linked model rather than cost-plus or hourly, which in principle lets an operator retain efficiency gains it captures until the next renewal. In practice retention is compressed by three documented forces: (1) intense, well-capitalized competition -- ADP, Paychex/Paycor, Paylocity, Paycom, plus Gusto/Rippling in the SMB tier, are all shipping comparable AI features, narrowing any single bureau's differentiation; (2) the emergence of embedded-payroll infrastructure (Check, Gusto Embedded, Zeal, Salsa, ADP Embedded) that lets vertical software platforms become the payroll provider directly, at similar per-employee pricing, undercutting the need for a standalone bureau relationship at renewal; (3) low structural switching costs for the simplest clients once a plan is set up, even though setup friction and an ongoing tax-liability relationship provide some stickiness. Volume/client-loss effects are excluded here and carried in d5/o; this is only about how much of a given implemented efficiency gain the operator keeps versus passes through in price.

caveats: No direct survey of AI-adoption timelines among independent payroll bureaus was found; this is bounded judgment built from adjacent evidence (vendor roadmaps, liability structure), not a measured implementation rate.

Faster large-vendor AI shipping could pull small-bureau adoption forward, or could instead accelerate disintermediation of bureaus entirely (captured in `o`) rather than being 'implemented' by the bureau itself; that risk is carried in `o`, not double-counted here.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor product-adoption disclosures (e.g., ADP/Paychex/Paylocity earnings-call commentary on AI-feature attach rates among small/mid bureau customers, or a direct survey of independent bureau owners on AI-tool adoption) would sharpen this; documented case studies of bureaus that have removed a specific human review step (e.g., first-pass tax-notice triage) would be direct evidence.
rho.rationale: LMM-band bureaus are overwhelmingly technology consumers, not builders: they run client payroll on top of ADP Workforce Now, Paychex, Paylocity, Paycom, Gusto, QuickBooks, or Kronos/UKG rails, all of which are publicly racing to ship AI features (anomaly detection, natural-language support, compliance copilots) into their core platforms. That lowers the technical lift for a small bureau to capture exposed opportunity, since it mostly means adopting vendor features rather than building models in-house. Counterweighing that: payroll processing carries personal trust-fund-tax liability for whoever is responsible for deposits and filings (the IRS Trust Fund Recovery Penalty reaches business owners, officers, payroll managers, bookkeepers, and controllers), which makes bureaus and their clients cautious about removing human review from tax-filing and deposit steps even where technically feasible; multi-state regulatory heterogeneity and dependence on third-party platform release cadence (rather than in-house control over the roadmap) also slow full implementation. Net, roughly half of the exposed opportunity is judged achievable operationally within five years.

s5.bridge: Source population #1 is public-accounting/CPA firm partner-age data (19th Annual Rosenberg Practice Management Survey, via CPA Practice Advisor): in the $2-10M revenue firm category, 65.5% of partners are over age 50, rising to 71.1% for sole practitioners and smaller firms, with AICPA projecting 75% of today's CPAs retiring within 15 years. Transformation: payroll-bureau owners in the LMM band are judged demographically similar to this population (many bureaus are founded/run by accountants, bookkeepers, or former corporate payroll managers of comparable career stage), used as a proxy for owner-age skew in 541214; bridge risk is that payroll-bureau founders may run younger on average, since the industry has a more recent wave of tech-enabled entrants than the CPA profession. Source population #2 is observed deal flow in the adjacent/overlapping payroll-and-PEO bureau roll-up space: Vensure Employer Solutions (PE-backed via Stone Point Capital, merged with PrismHR) has completed 108 acquisitions of small payroll/HR service providers as of 2026, up from 75 six years prior, explicitly described as reducing the number of independent payroll companies, typically structured as 80% acquirer / 20% seller-rollover. Transformation: used as a proxy for buyer-side deal propensity across the lens population; error direction is ambiguous -- Vensure's targets skew toward PEO/ASO (co-employment) models more than pure 541214 payroll processing, which could overstate deal receptiveness for stand-alone bureaus that never took on PEO risk, but the scale and cadence of just this one acquirer (not counting Paychex/Paycor, ADP, or other PE-backed regional platforms) likely understates total market deal flow.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is drawn from CPA/accounting firms, not payroll bureaus specifically -- a population proxy, not a direct measurement.

Deal-flow evidence is drawn substantially from one acquirer (Vensure/PrismHR) whose targets blend PEO/ASO and payroll-processing business models; the pure-541214 slice of that activity is not separately reported.

The Rosenberg survey figures are from a 2018 publication; more recent AICPA retirement-wave commentary suggests the skew has not reversed, but current-year percentages are not independently confirmed here.
s5.change_evidence: A direct survey or trade-association (e.g., National Payroll Reporting Consortium, PayrollOrg member survey) of owner age and succession intentions among independent 541214 bureaus; a deal-count tally across all active acquirers (Vensure, Paychex/Paycor, ADP, PE-backed regional roll-ups, business brokers listing payroll bureaus specifically) rather than one acquirer's public press releases.
s5.rationale: Two independent signals both point toward an above-average five-year transfer probability for this industry relative to a generic small-business base rate: an aging, accounting-adjacent owner population, and unusually active, already-proven roll-up buyers explicitly targeting small payroll/HR service providers at a double-digit-per-year acquisition pace. Set below the top of the range because the owner-age proxy is imperfect (CPA firms, not payroll bureaus) and because a meaningful share of the documented buyer activity is PEO-flavored rather than pure payroll-processing-bureau M&A.

principal_driver: Durable, regulation-driven multi-state payroll-tax complexity sustains demand for an accountable third-party operator even as the underlying processing task automates.
principal_weakness: Thin, competitively-shopped per-employee-per-month pricing lets larger platforms and embedded-payroll infrastructure compete away most of any automation-driven efficiency gain before an LMM-band operator can retain it.
